title: DSA private keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don't need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.pattern: -----BEGIN DSA PRIVATE KEY-----[\s\S]*?-----END DSA PRIVATE KEY-----
checks.message: DSA Private Key detected in PEM format. DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm) private keys are used for digital signatures and SSH authentication. Exposing this key allows attackers to impersonate you for SSH access to servers, forge digital signatures, decrypt communications encrypted with the corresponding public key, and gain unauthorized access to any system where this key is authorized. Delete the key from repositories immediately and generate a new key pair.

checks.pattern: -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----[\s\S]*?-----END OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----
checks.message: OpenSSH Private Key detected. OpenSSH private keys provide authentication to SSH servers and services. When this is a DSA key, exposing it enables impersonation and unauthorized access to all servers where the public key is authorized. Even non-DSA OpenSSH keys represent critical credential exposure. Generate a new key pair immediately and remove the old public key from all authorized_keys files.

PKCS#8 Private Key detected (may be DSA). This unencrypted private key in PKCS#8 format could be a DSA key used for authentication or digital signing. Exposing private keys enables impersonation, unauthorized system access, and signature forgery. Remove from source code immediately, rotate all associated credentials, and store keys securely using proper key management.
